title: Quiz: CPTP Maps
description: Test your understanding of completely positive trace-preserving maps

questions.question: What does CPTP stand for?
questions.explanation: CPTP stands for Completely Positive Trace-Preserving, the mathematical requirements for valid quantum channels.

questions.question: Which map is positive but NOT completely positive?
questions.explanation: The transpose map T(rho) = rho^T is positive but not completely positive. Applied to half of an entangled state, it can produce non-positive operators.

questions.question: A positive map always takes density matrices to density matrices, even when applied to part of a larger system.
questions.explanation: False! Positive maps preserve positivity only when applied to the entire system. Complete positivity is needed for subsystems.

questions.question: The Choi matrix J(E) of a CPTP map E satisfies:
questions.explanation: For CPTP maps: CP requires J(E) >= 0, and TP requires the partial trace over output system equals I/d.

questions.question: The Stinespring dilation theorem states that every CPTP map can be written as:

options.text: An isometry followed by partial trace

questions.explanation: Stinespring: E(rho) = Tr_E[V rho V^dag] where V is an isometry to a larger space including an environment.

questions.question: Why is complete positivity (not just positivity) required for quantum channels?

options.text: Quantum systems can be entangled with ancillas
It is required by the uncertainty principle

questions.explanation: Quantum systems can be entangled with ancillas (reference systems). A channel acting on one part must preserve positivity of the joint state.

questions.question: For the depolarizing channel D_p(rho) = (1-p)rho + p*I/d, what is the physical interpretation?

options.text: State becomes completely random with probability p

questions.explanation: The depolarizing channel replaces the state with the maximally mixed state I/d with probability p, representing complete randomization.

questions.question: The composition of two CPTP maps is always CPTP.
questions.explanation: True. CPTP maps form a semigroup under composition. If E1 and E2 are CPTP, then E2 o E1 is also CPTP.
